is really this idea of like AI cybersecurity or.

So in terms of your competitive differentiation when it comes to AI, what does SentinelOne do that maybe we should be highlighting more to capture like these LLM like referrals?
Yeah, so a few different things.
mean, I think Fathomar would say that we're really the only We're the only ones that are truly.
Using AI, whereas others are not.
But I think the, and we're sort of, we're the first ones to do it and we're natively AI.
Generally, I find the argument of like, we did something first to be pretty weak because it means that you don't have something better to talk about rather than say like, we're the best platform.
Yeah, so, but I think that is something that he would push for.
I think where we are differentiated in our capabilities is practically on sort of the endpoint side.

We're pulling in all of the signals in terms of what people are doing and we're using sort of behavioral AI to be able to say like, oh, this seems atypical based on sort of, you know, someone's coming in and they're moving laterally within your environment.
That seems like a problem.
And so it's sort of like that discovery leveraging AI because it's sort of getting new signals and balancing it back.
And then has the ability to sort of auto-remediate based on that.
So that's sort of like one area.
The second one, and the one that we spend more time now on, because that other one we've had for quite a bit, is we can essentially take all of your data, so security-related data.

So it could be from endpoint, it could be general sort of logs, and then being able to pull it into a singular place, and then using sort of generative AI and sort of natural language to be able to sort of query those things really quickly.

And not only use it to sort of identify things that you might not have been able to do if you were just sort of doing sort of broad-based search, but then able to sort of then actually leverage sort of the AI to also auto-remediate in sort of a much broader scale.

And so I'd say that's the thing right now that's probably the most differentiating, because there's no one who can effectively pull that data together at sort of the cost that we can or at the speed that we can, and then leverage generative AI to search it through quickly, and then sort of leverage AI to sort of solve problems.
And so I'd
Like a Splunk-type replacement in terms of the capabilities.
And then the sort of the next thing that we have that are sort of working on more is to leverage AI to do all of the normalization and matching of the data.

Because one of the things that tends to be a problem in our space, but in general in the data space, is how do you understand sort of like this piece of data, that piece of data, and how it naturally like fits together.
Unless you have just sort of very like clear tables that are fully exhaustive, and leveraging AI to sort of assist in the matching process.

And then I would say is that probably the biggest thing that's getting folks excited, the sort of the next step that we're working on is how to very quick and efficiently actually then migrate everything out of the other capabilities, taking advantage of AI.
